For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. {{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Public holidays]][[Category:11]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Public holidays]][[Category:11]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Public holidays]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

{{Otheruseslist|[[1948年]]7月19日以前の日本における祝祭日|[[1948年]]7月20日以降の日本における祝日|国民の祝日|その他の祝日|祝日|その他の祭日|祭日}} [[1948年]]7月19日以前の日本における'''祝祭日'''（しゅくさいじつ）では、慣習により規律され、または法令により規定された[[祝日]]および[[祭日]]について述べる。 なお、「祝祭日」とは一般には[[日本]]において[[祝日]]と[[祭日]]のふたつを総称する言葉である。 == 概要 == === 安土桃山時代以前 === {{Empty section}} === 江戸期 === 以下のような祝祭日があった。五[[節句]]は、[[中国]]の[[陰陽五行思想|陰陽五行説]]に由来して定着した[[日本の暦|日本の]]伝統的な[[年中行事]]を行う[[日本の文化]]・[[風習]]である。[[江戸幕府|幕府]]が公的な行事・[[祝日]]として定めた{{Cite web|和書|title=日建協：特集 祝日と日本人のお話|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=2019-04-02|publisher=日本建設産業職員労働組合協議会}}。 {{節スタブ|date=2019年4月2日 (火) 19:24 (UTC)}} === 明治期 - 戦前期 === 維新後[[明治]]期の国威発揚政策の一環として旧来の五節句などを廃止して、[[国家神道]]推進のために規定された。「[[年中祭日祝日ノ休暇日ヲ定ム]]」（明治6年[[太政官布告・太政官達|太政官布告]]第344号、[[1873年]][[10月14日]]施行、[[1878年]][[6月5日]]・[[1879年]][[7月5日]]改正）によって、[[大正]]期・[[昭和]]初期には「[[休日ニ関スル件]]」（大正元年[[勅令]]第19号、[[1912年]][[9月3日]]施行、[[1913年]][[7月16日]]・[[1927年]][[3月3日]]改正）によって定められていた。 どちらの休日法にも「祭日」と表記され、「大祭日」との表記は無いが、[[皇室祭祀]]における「[[大祭]]」をベースにしていることから「祝日大祭日」略して「祝祭日」と呼ばれる。大祭のうち[[式年祭|式年]]のものを除く毎年のもの全てが該当するが、皇霊祭と同日の神殿祭は省略されている。また、紀元節祭・天長節祭は「祭」をとって紀元節・天長節となり祝日に分類されている。なお、天長節祭は唯一「[[小祭]]」である。その他の新年宴会と明治節は皇室祭祀ではない。 祝意を表し各家に国旗が掲げられたことから、明治から大正期にかけて一般的に「旗日」とも呼ばれていた。 [[1948年]][[7月20日]]、「[[国民の祝日に関する法律]]」（祝日法）の施行によって「休日ニ関スル件」が廃止された。 == 江戸期の祝祭日 == {{節スタブ|date=2019年4月2日 (火) 19:24 (UTC)}} * [[元旦]] * [[人日]]（七草の節句） * [[上巳]]（桃の節句） * [[端午]]（端午の節句） * [[嘉祥 (行事)|嘉定]]（嘉祥） * [[七夕]] * [[八朔]]（田の実の節句） * [[重陽]]（菊の節句） * [[亥の子餅|玄猪]] == 明治 - 戦前期の祝祭日 == === 祝祭日一覧 === * 祝（祭）日名〜適用終了年欄の背景が灰色のものは、期日が変更されるなどして適用されなくなった祝日である。 * 適用開始年欄に「制定時」と記載のあるものは、[[1873年]][[10月14日]]の法律制定当初から存在する祝祭日である。適用終了年欄に「-」と記載のあるものは祝日法施行まで施行されていたことを示す。 * 改正により追加された祝日の適用開始年欄には、実際に初めて適用された年を記載する。法令上その祝日に関する規定が発効した年とは必ずしも一致しない。 * 改正により期日が変更された祝日の適用開始年欄には期日変更前も含めた最初の適用年を記載し、変更年は備考欄に記載する。 * 祝日法施行までの最後の祝祭日は1948年[[4月29日]]（天長節）である。 ==== 祝日 ==== {| class="wikitable" !祝日名!!期日!!style="white-space:nowrap"|適用開始年!!style="white-space:nowrap"|適用終了年!!備考 |- |style="white-space:nowrap"|（[[元日|四方節]]）||[[1月1日]]||colspan="2"|-||法令で定められてはいない。年初の慣習による。のちの[[元日]]（[[元旦]]） |- |[[新年宴会]]||[[1月5日]]||rowspan="2"|制定時||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[紀元節]]||[[2月11日]]||のちの[[建国記念の日]]（[[1967年]]〜） |- |rowspan="2"|[[天皇誕生日#近代・現代|天長節]]||[[4月29日]]||[[1927年]]||[[大正天皇]][[崩御]]により、[[昭和天皇]]誕生日へ移行。のちの[[天皇誕生日]]（〜[[1988年]]）→[[みどりの日]]（[[1989年|1989]]〜[[2006年]]）→[[昭和の日]]（[[2007年]]〜） |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[8月31日]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[1913年]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[1926年]]||[[明治天皇]][[崩御]]により、大正天皇誕生日へ移行 |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[天皇誕生日#近代・現代|天長節祝日]]||style="background-color:#dddddd; white-space:nowrap"|[[10月31日]]||大正天皇誕生日が盛暑期であるため、2ヶ月後も天長節の祝日とした |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|天長節||rowspan="2"|[[11月3日]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|制定時||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[1911年]]||のちの明治節（[[1927年]]〜） |- |[[明治節]]||1927年||-||のちの[[文化の日]] |} 昭和天皇が即位した[[1927年]]に[[明治節]]が制定されると、このうち新年宴会を除く4つの祝日（四方節、紀元節、天長節、明治節）を'''四大節'''（しだいせつ）と呼んだ。 ==== 祭日 ==== {| class="wikitable" !祭日名!!期日!!適用開始年!!適用終了年!!備考 |- |[[元始祭]]||[[1月3日]]||制定時||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[孝明天皇祭]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[1月30日]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|制定時||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[1912年]]||明治天皇崩御により廃止 |- |春季[[皇霊祭]]||[[春分]]日
（[[3月21日]]ごろ）||[[1879年]]||rowspan="2"|-||rowspan="2"|  |- |[[神武天皇祭]]||[[4月3日]]||制定時 |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[明治天皇祭]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[7月30日]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913年||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926年||明治天皇崩御により施行、大正天皇崩御により廃止 |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|神嘗祭||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[9月17日]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|制定時||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[1878年]]||10月17日に移行する形で廃止 |- |秋季皇霊祭||[[秋分]]日
（[[9月23日]]ごろ）||1878年||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |[[神嘗祭]]||[[10月17日]]||[[1879年]]||9月17日から移行する形で施行 |- |[[新嘗祭]]||[[11月23日]]||制定時||のちの[[勤労感謝の日]] |- |[[大正天皇祭]]||[[12月25日]]||1927年||大正天皇崩御により施行 |} === 皇室慶弔行事に伴う祭日 === [[皇室]]関係の慶弔行事が行われる場合は、その年に限りそれが実施される日を特別に休日として定める法律が作られる。その際には、その休日を「休日ニ關スル件」の休日と同等なものとして扱うよう附則で定めるのが通例であった。国・地方自治体ではこれに沿った取扱いがなされた。 * [[1915年]] ** [[11月10日]]（[[水曜日|水]]） - [[即位の礼]] ** [[11月14日]]（[[日曜日|日]]） - [[大嘗祭]] ** [[11月16日]]（[[火曜日|火]]） - [[大饗]]第1日 * [[1928年]] ** 11月10日（[[土曜日|土]]） - 即位の礼 ** 11月14日（水） - 大嘗祭 ** 11月16日（[[金曜日|金]]） - 大饗第1日 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[国民の祝日]] * [[祝日]] * [[祭日]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:戦前日本の祝祭日|*]] {{DEFAULTSORT:しゆくさいしつ}}

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-Public holidays-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Public holidays]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. {{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. ({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox

For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Public holidays]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ublic holidays]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|江戸幕府}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[ja:祝祭日]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} [[Category:bump]] [[Category:National holidays]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Shukusai]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Public holidays]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ublic holidays]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:祝祭日]]

The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites.
