{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:National holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. ({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=draft}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{citation needed|しゆくさいしつ}} {{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}}{{citation needed|Shukusai}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:National holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." {{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:National holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōmei Emperor Festival|qq=}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōreisai|qq=}}||Spring Equinox

({{ill|September 23|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 23|qq=}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisho Festival|qq=}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:National holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "陰陽五行思想|" to "陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|"
revision.text:
{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosekku|qq=}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual events|qq=}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's Day|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinjitsu|qq=}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakusa no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Third Festival|qq=}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momo no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon Boat Festival|qq=}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō (festival)|qq=}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku|qq=}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta no Mi no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|qq=}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiku no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoko mochi|qq=}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihohai|qq=}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Foundation Day|qq=}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōmei Emperor Festival|qq=}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōreisai|qq=}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 21|qq=}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 23|qq=}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisho Festival|qq=}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:National holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:National holidays]]
revision.text:
{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosekku|qq=}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual events|qq=}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's Day|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinjitsu|qq=}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakusa no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Third Festival|qq=}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momo no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon Boat Festival|qq=}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō (festival)|qq=}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku|qq=}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta no Mi no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|qq=}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiku no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoko mochi|qq=}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihohai|qq=}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. Later became [[:en:New_Year’s_Day|New Year’s Day]] (元旦). |- |[[:ja:新年宴会|New Year’s Banquet]]||[[January 5]]||rowspan="2"|At the time of legislation||rowspan="3"|-|| |- |[[National Foundation Day]]||[[February 11]]||Later became {{ill|National Foundation Day|ja|建国記念の日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Foundation Day|qq=}} (1967–). |- |rowspan="2"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]||[[April 29]]||[[1927]]||Shifted to Showa Emperor’s Birthday following the death of Emperor Taisho. Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōmei Emperor Festival|qq=}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōreisai|qq=}}||Spring Equinox
({{ill|March 21|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 21|qq=}} approx.)||[[1879]]||rowspan="2"|-|| |- |[[Emperor Jimmu Festival]]||[[April 3]]||At the time of legislation|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|Emperor Meiji Festival||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[July 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Established after Emperor Meiji’s death; abolished after Emperor Taisho’s death. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[September 17]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1878||Shifted to October 17. |- |Autumn Kōrei-sai||Autumn Equinox
({{ill|September 23|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 23|qq=}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisho Festival|qq=}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Shukusai]] [[Category:Prewar Japanese Holidays]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosekku|qq=}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual events|qq=}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta no Mi no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiku no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoko mochi|qq=}}** == Later became [[:en:Shōwa_Day|Showa Day]] (2007–). |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[August 31]]||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1913||rowspan="2" style="background-color:#dddddd"|1926||Shifted to Taisho Emperor’s Birthday. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[October 31]]||Holiday two months after the actual birthday to avoid midsummer heat. |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|[[The Emperor's Birthday]]|| rowspan="2" |[[November 3]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1911||Later became {{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}} (1927–). |- |{{ill|Meiji Day|ja|明治節|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Day|qq=}}||1927||-||Later became [[:en:Culture_Day|Culture Day]]. |} ==== Festivals ==== {| class="wikitable" !Festival Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |[[Genshi-sai]]||[[January 3]]||At the time of legislation||-|| |- |style="background-color:#dddddd"|{{ill|Kōmei Emperor Festival|ja|孝明天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōmei Emperor Festival|qq=}}|| style="background-color:#dddddd" |[[January 30]]||style="background-color:#dddddd"|At the time of legislation||style="background-color:#dddddd"|1912||Discontinued after Emperor Meiji’s death. |- |Spring {{ill|Kōreisai|ja|皇霊祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōreisai|qq=}}||Spring Equinox

({{ill|September 23|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 23|qq=}} approx.)||1878||rowspan="4"|-|| |- |{{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai Festival|qq=}}||[[October 17]]||[[1879]]||Shifted from September 17. |- |[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]]||[[November 23]]||At the time of legislation||Later became [[Labor Thanksgiving Day]]. |- |{{ill|Taisho Festival|ja|大正天皇祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisho Festival|qq=}}||[[December 25]]||1927||Established after Emperor Taisho’s death. |} === Imperial Family Celebrations === Special holidays were legislated for important imperial events and treated equivalently to the public holidays under the "Regulations on Public Holidays." For example: * 1915 ** [[November 10]] (Wednesday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** [[November 14]] (Sunday) – Daijosai ** [[November 16]] (Tuesday) – First Daikyō Day * 1928 ** November 10 (Saturday) – Enthronement Ceremony ** November 14 (Wednesday) – Daijosai ** November 16 (Friday) – First Daikyō Day == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:Public_holidays_in_Japan|National Holidays]] * [[:en:Holiday|Holidays]] * [[Festivals]] {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|祝祭日|version=104117640|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Holidays in Pre-War Japan]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Public holidays]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:祝祭日]] [[en:Public holidays]] [[sn:Public holidays]] [[sn:ublic holidays]]

{{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The {{ill|Gosekku|ja|節句|lt=five Sekku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosekku|qq=}} were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual events|qq=}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}}. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's Day|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Jinjitsu|ja|人日|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinjitsu|qq=}}** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakusa no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Third Festival|ja|上巳|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Third Festival|qq=}}** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momo no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|2=ja|3=端午|4=fr|5=Festival des bateaux-dragons|6=ru|7=Праздник драконьих лодок|8=es|9=Fiesta del Barco del Dragón|10=nl|11=Drakenbootfeest|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon Boat Festival|qq=}}** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Kashō (festival)|ja|嘉祥|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō (festival)|qq=}}** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Tanabata|ja|七夕|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}}** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku|qq=}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta no Mi no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Double Ninth Festival|ja|重陽|1=Double Ninth Festival|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|13=User:Immanuelle/Double Ninth Festival|qq=}}** – Chrysanthemum Festival ({{ill|Kiku no Sekku|ja|菊の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiku no Sekku|qq=}}) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoko mochi|qq=}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihohai|qq=}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday. {{Otheruseslist|Holidays and festivals in Japan before July 19, 1948|Holidays in Japan after July 20, 1948|National Holidays|Other holidays|Holidays|Other festivals|Festivals}} '''Holidays and Festivals''' (祝祭日, Shukusaijitsu) in Japan before July 19, 1948, were either regulated by custom or prescribed by law. This article discusses such holidays (祝日, Shukujitsu) and festivals (祭日, Saijitsu) that existed during this period. Notably, "Holidays and Festivals" is a term commonly used in Japan to refer collectively to both holidays and festivals. == Overview == === Before the Azuchi-Momoyama Period === {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Edo Period === During the Edo period, the following holidays and festivals were celebrated. The [[:en:Gosekku|five Sekku]] were traditional {{ill|annual events|ja|年中行事|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/annual events|qq=}} rooted in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽五行思想|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|1=Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|13=User:Immanuelle/Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory|qq=}} from China, which became part of Japan’s culture and customs. The {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa shogunate|lt=Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Shogunate|qq=}} designated these as public ceremonies and holidays.{{Cite web|title=Nikkenkyo: The Story of Holidays and Japanese People|url=http://nikkenkyo.jp/before/back_number/compass781/08-09holiday/index.htm|website=nikkenkyo.jp|accessdate=April 2, 2019|publisher=Japan Construction Industry Staff Union Council}} {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} === Meiji to Pre-War Period === After the Meiji Restoration, traditional holidays such as the five Sekku were abolished as part of efforts to enhance national prestige. Instead, holidays were codified to promote [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]]. The holidays were defined by the "Regulations on Annual Festivals, Holidays, and Days Off" (明治6年太政官布告第344号), promulgated on October 14, 1873, and later revised in 1878 and 1879. Subsequent revisions, including the "Regulations on Public Holidays" (大正元年勅令第19号), implemented in 1912 and revised in 1913 and 1927, further shaped the holiday system. Although the term "festival days" (祭日) appeared in these regulations, "Great Festivals" (大祭日) was not used. However, most festivals were based on imperial rites classified as "Shukujitsu Taisai" (祝祭大祭日), abbreviated as "Shukusaijitsu." Annual rites, excluding periodic ones, were included, but shrine rites such as the Shrine Festival (神殿祭) were omitted. Furthermore, holidays like National Foundation Day (紀元節) and the Emperor's Birthday (天長節) omitted the "Festival" suffix and were classified as holidays. The Emperor's Birthday, uniquely, was a "minor festival" (小祭). Other events, such as the New Year's Banquet and Meiji Day, were not part of imperial rites. Because of the tradition of raising the national flag at homes to mark celebrations, these holidays were colloquially called "flag days" during the Meiji and Taisho periods. The "Regulations on Public Holidays" were repealed on July 20, 1948, following the enactment of the "Public Holiday Law" (国民の祝日に関する法律). == Holidays and Festivals in the Edo Period == {{Section-stub|date=April 2, 2019}} * **{{ill|New Year's Day|ja|元旦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Year's Day|qq=}}** * **[[:en:Jinjitsu|Jinjitsu]]** – Festival of the Seven Herbs ({{ill|Nanakusa no Sekku|ja|七草の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanakusa no Sekku|qq=}}) * **[[:en:Double Third Festival|Double Third Festival]]** – Peach Festival ({{ill|Momo no Sekku|ja|桃の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momo no Sekku|qq=}}) * **[[:en:Dragon Boat Festival|Dragon Boat Festival]]** – Boy’s Festival ({{ill|Tango no Sekku|ja|端午の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|qq=}}) * **[[:en:Kashō|Kashō (festival)]]** – Kashō Festival ({{ill|Kashō|ja|嘉定|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashō|qq=}}) * **[[:en:Qixi Festival|Tanabata]]** * **{{ill|Hassaku|ja|八朔|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku|qq=}}** – Rice Harvest Festival ({{ill|Ta no Mi no Sekku|ja|田の実の節句|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta no Mi no Sekku|qq=}}) * **[[:en:Double Ninth Festival|Double Ninth Festival]]** – Chrysanthemum Festival ([[:en:Chrysanthemum Day|Kiku no Sekku]]) * **{{ill|Inoko mochi|ja|玄猪|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoko mochi|qq=}}** == Holidays and Festivals in the Meiji to Pre-War Period == === List of Holidays and Festivals === * Holidays and festivals with gray backgrounds in the "End Year" column indicate they were discontinued due to changes in dates or other reasons. * Holidays listed as "At the time of legislation" in the "Start Year" column were introduced on October 14, 1873, when the regulations were first enacted. Holidays with a "-" in the "End Year" column lasted until the implementation of the Public Holiday Law. * For holidays added through revisions, the "Start Year" reflects the year they were first celebrated. * Holidays with date changes include their original implementation year in the "Start Year" column, with changes noted in the remarks. * The last holiday before the enactment of the Public Holiday Law was April 29, 1948 (the Emperor’s Birthday). ==== Holidays ==== {| class="wikitable" !Holiday Name!!Date!!Start Year!!End Year!!Remarks |- |{{ill|Shihohai|ja|四方節|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihohai|qq=}}||[[January 1]]||colspan="2"|-||Customary New Year’s Day holiday.
